items.q: How do hormones reach their target organs?
items.correct: They are carried in the bloodstream and act on cells with specific receptors for that hormone.
items.misconception: Hormones are sent through nerves directly to specific organs.
items.random_wrong: Hormones are released directly into target organs by being injected by glands.
items.why: Students conflate nervous and endocrine signalling; they think every signal in the body travels through nerves.

items.q: What does insulin do to blood glucose?
items.correct: It lowers blood glucose by causing cells (especially liver and muscle) to take it up and store it as glycogen.
items.misconception: Insulin raises blood glucose to give the body energy.
items.random_wrong: Insulin destroys glucose in the bloodstream so it cannot be used.
items.why: Students associate sugar with energy and 'doing something with sugar' with raising energy, missing that insulin removes glucose from blood.

items.q: Why is a reflex action faster than a conscious response?
items.correct: The signal does not need to be processed by the brain — it travels through a simpler reflex arc via the spinal cord.
items.misconception: The brain decides reflexes faster than other actions because it skips thinking.
items.random_wrong: Reflexes are faster because they use specially fast neurones not used elsewhere.
items.why: Students assume the brain is always in charge; they don't realise reflexes specifically bypass the brain to save time.

items.q: Are hormonal responses just as fast as nervous responses?
items.correct: No — hormonal responses are slower to start but longer-lasting; nervous responses are fast and brief.
items.misconception: Hormones act as quickly as nerve impulses.
items.random_wrong: Hormones travel along neurones to reach their targets.
items.why: Students conflate the systems, but hormones travel in the blood (slower, widespread) while nerve impulses are rapid electrical signals along neurones.

items.correct: No — insulin lowers blood glucose by causing cells to take it up and store it as glycogen; glucagon raises it.
items.misconception: Insulin raises blood glucose to provide energy.
items.random_wrong: Insulin destroys glucose so it cannot be used.
items.why: Students assume a glucose-related hormone increases glucose, but insulin reduces it; its opposite, glucagon, is the one that raises blood glucose.

items.q: Compared to nervous signals, hormonal signals are usually.
items.why: It's Slower and longer-lasting. “Faster and shorter” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which gland is known as the 'master gland'?
items.why: It's Pituitary. “Thyroid” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a person has 2 ovaries, 2 adrenal glands, 1 thyroid, 1 pituitary and 1 pancreas, how many of these endocrine glands do they have in total?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
